QUESTION: What is the role of the Singapore Prison Service in the rehabilitation of inmates?
CONTEXT: The Singapore Prison Service (SPS) was instituted in 1946 as a government department. However, the origins of Singaporeâ€™s penal system can be traced to the arrival of the first batch of convicts in 1825. The philosophy of deterrence through punitive measures was adopted in the prisons then, which were managed by colonial administrators, until the Second World War. In 1946, Prisons Department, now known as the Singapore Prison Service, was formally instituted as a government department to oversee the management of prisons. Over the years, SPS has progressed to become a leading correctional agency, enforcing safe and secure custody, delivering evidence-informed rehabilitation programmes and carrying out effective community supervision of released inmates. SPS and its staff, also known as Captains of Lives, have since undergone a transformation journey, and its community partners have been instrumental in this journey. In 2021, SPS celebrated SPS75, commemorating 75 years since its inception as a government agency. SPS75 was officially launched on 21 June 2021 by Minister for Communications and Information and Second Minister for Home Affairs, Mrs Josephine Teo during SPS-YRSG Corporate Advance 2021. It was also commemorated during the SPS75 Virtual Commemorative Event with the President of Singapore, Mdm Halimah Yacob as the Guest-of-Honour. The SPS75 Commemoration Event was also attended by former staff and Director / Commissioner of Prisons, as well as its partners, volunteers and overseas counterparts. Our Journey To Transformation 1946-1965: Prisons Pre-Independence 1946 G. E. W. W. Bayly, First Commissioner of Prisons When the British Military Administration was replaced by a civil government, Singapore became a Crown Colony and the Prisons Department (now known as Singapore Prison Service) was instituted as a government agency. G. E. W. W. Bayly became the first Commissioner of Prisons after the Second World War. The photo shows the first logo of then Prisons Department. The insignia reflects Prisons Department as an agency in the Straits settlement under the purview of the Crown Colony.      1953 Major W.L.P. Sochon, Second Commissioner of Prisons W.L.P. Sochon took over as the second Commissioner of Prisons. He was supportive of the idea of a 'prison without bars' which relied on companies and the public doing their part to accept inmates into society. The photo shows Major W.L.P Sochon holding a press conference cum tour of the new experimental opium rehabilitation centre on St John's Island.      Source: Ministry of Information and the Arts Collection, courtesy of National Archives of Singapore 1955 Source: Ministry of Information and the Arts Collection, courtesy of National Archives of Singapore St Johnâ€™s Island Opium Treatment Centre The first Drug Rehabilitation Centre (DRC) in Singapore was the Opium Treatment Centre (OTC) at St. Johnâ€™s Island. It was subsequently repurposed and expanded to receive other drug abusers. The OTC consisted of 12 huts, of which six were used as accommodation for up to 40 inmates. The others were used for workshops as shown in the photo.      1957 P. L. James, Third Commissioner of Prisons P. L. James served as the third Commissioner of Prisons up until the merger between Singapore and Malaysia between 1957 to 1963. He was known to grant political detainees leave on Chinese New Year.      Source: The Straits Times Â© Singapore Press Holdings Limited. Permission required for reproduction. 1959 Source: National Archives of Singapore Prison Inquiry Commission The Prisons Inquiry Commission was convened and chaired by Devan Nair who strongly recommended that the Prisons Department should shift its focus from custodial care to rehabilitation. The photo shows Devan Nair presenting the Report of the Prison Inquiry Commission to Yang Di-Pertuan Negara Yusof Ishak.      1960 Pulau Senang Penal Settlement Pulau Senang Penal Settlement started as an experimental rehabilitative facility for inmates from Changi Prison. It was closed after a riot on 12 July 1963. The photo shows inmates arriving in Pulau Senang in the early 1960s.      Source: National Archives of Singapore 1961 Source: Ministry of Information and the Arts Collection, courtesy of National Archives of Singapore Bedok Reformative Training Centre The Bedok Reformative Training Centre (BRTC) was established as a centre for young male offenders, between 16 and 21 years old, to undergo reformative training. The photo shows the facility of BRTC.      1963 Frankie Lee Siew Kwong, Acting Director of Prisons Frankie Lee Siew Kwong served as the Acting Director of Prisons during Singapore's merger with Malaysia. As the former Administrative Officer of Changi Prison, he was well-acquainted with the operation and administration of the penal system
ANSWER: The Singapore Prison Service plays a leading role in the rehabilitation of inmates by delivering evidence-informed rehabilitation programmes that focus on safe and secure custody and effective community supervision after release. It has transformed its approach from punitive measures to a more rehabilitative model, as highlighted by initiatives such as the shift recommended by the Prisons Inquiry Commission and the establishment of facilities like the Bedok Reformative Training Centre. Through these efforts, the SPS aims to help inmates reintegrate into society successfully.

It was later closed, and inmates were transferred to Admiralty West Prison which was completed in 2000.      1998 Chua Chin Kiat, Seventh Director of Prison After helming multiple Police
ANSWER: Singapore introduced vocational training programs through the Singapore Corporation of Rehabilitative Enterprises (SCORE), which later became Yellow Ribbon Singapore, to enhance the employability of offenders. The establishment of the Industrial and Services Co-operative Society Ltd (ISCOS) provided discharged inmates with job opportunities and small business support. Additionally, drug rehabilitation centers and specialized treatment facilities like the Falkland Centre were created to address substance abuse and support offenders in reintegrating into society.

QUESTION: What is the role of the Singapore Prison Service within the Ministry of Home Affairs?
CONTEXT: Singapore Prison Service (SPS), a uniformed organisation instituted in 1946, is one of the 11 Agencies under the  Ministry of Home Affairs  (MHA). Together, we form the Home Team. SPS contributes to MHAâ€™s Vision of being â€˜A Trusted Home Team, A Safe and Secure Singaporeâ€™ through the secure custody and rehabilitation of offenders. SPS staff, who are known as  Captains of Lives  (COLs), are highly engaged and work in an organisation recognised as one of Singaporeâ€™s Best Employers.  SPSâ€™s tagline: â€˜Rehab, Renew, Restartâ€™ emphasises our commitment to rehabilitate offenders who desire to change, renew and restart their lives for the better, with the support of the community. Organisation Structure SPS currently administers 14 institutions, Selarang Park Community Supervision Centre (SPCSC) and Community Rehabilitation Centre (CRC). They make up SPSâ€™s line units and are grouped under five Commands: Cluster A Cluster B Cluster C Community Corrections Command Operations & Security Command At the staff level, SPS has 11 Divisions: Corporate Communications & Service Division Intelligence Division Investigation Division Logistics & Finance Division Operations Division Psychological & Correctional Rehabilitation Division Rehabilitation & Reintegration Division Risk Management & Assurance Division Staff Development Division Strategic Planning Division Transformation & Technology Division
ANSWER: The Singapore Prison Service plays a key role within the Ministry of Home Affairs by securing the custody of offenders and providing rehabilitation programs to help them reform and restart their lives. As part of the Home Team, SPS contributes to the Ministry’s vision of being "A Trusted Home Team, A Safe and Secure Singapore" through its focus on rehabilitation, reintegration, and community support. Its mission, encapsulated in the tagline "Rehab, Renew, Restart," emphasizes helping offenders change and become productive members of society.

QUESTION: What is the mission of the Singapore Prison Service?
CONTEXT: Vision As Captains of Lives, we inspire everyone, at every chance, towards a society without re-offending. Mission As a correctional agency, we enforce secure custody of offenders and rehabilitate them, for a safe Singapore. Core Values Our Captains of Lives enforce the secure custody of inmates and provide opportunities for them to rehabilitate to become better individuals. With the support from our community partners and volunteers, we also provide support to inmatesâ€™ families and facilitate the inmatesâ€™ reintegration into society. We are guided by a set of core values -   Synergy, Vigilance, Integrity and Care (SVIC) .
ANSWER: The mission of the Singapore Prison Service is to enforce secure custody of offenders and rehabilitate them for a safe Singapore.

QUESTION: What role does the Singapore Prison Service play in the rehabilitation of inmates?
CONTEXT: Our Role as Captains of Lives Our Captains of Lives keep Singapore safe by ensuring the secure custody of inmates in an impartial and humane manner, while providing rehabilitation opportunities to them. In our pursuit to be an exemplary correctional agency, we maintain the highest standards of order and discipline. We also provide  rehabilitation   programmes for inmates  to achieve the best outcomes in recidivism rates. We help inmates take ownership of their own rehabilitation, by positively influencing them to  renew  their family bonds and reintegrating into society. Our role extends beyond the prison walls. We work closely with our  community partners  to support and guide ex-offenders as they take steps to  restart  their lives. Our Captains of Lives are mentors, coaches and role models to those under our charge.
ANSWER: The Singapore Prison Service plays a crucial role in the rehabilitation of inmates by providing structured rehabilitation programmes aimed at reducing recidivism. It helps inmates take ownership of their rehabilitation by encouraging the renewal of family bonds and promoting reintegration into society. The service also works with community partners to support ex-offenders in restarting their lives, acting as mentors, coaches, and role models throughout the process.

QUESTION: What is the role of the Yellow Ribbon Project in Singapore's rehabilitation efforts for ex-offenders?
CONTEXT: A Low and Stable Recidivism Rate Singapore Prison Service (SPS) enforces the safe and secure custody of inmates, and rehabilitates them to prevent re-offending for a safe Singapore. Strong community partnerships and support for second chances have helped keep the two-year recidivism rates in Singapore low and stable. The two-year recidivism rate for the 2019 release cohort is 20 per cent, down from about 44 per cent two decades ago. Yellow Ribbon Project (YRP)   Started in 2004, the  Yellow Ribbon Project  is a community initiative in Singapore that aims to raise awareness on the need to give second chances to ex-offenders, generate acceptance of ex-offenders and their families in the community and inspire community action to support the rehabilitation and reintegration of ex-offenders into society.Â  The Yellow Ribbon Project is an initiative under the Community Action for the Rehabilitation of Ex-Offenders (CARE) Network, which comprises: Industrial & Services Co-operative Society Ltd  (ISCOS) Ministry of Home Affairs  (MHA) Ministry of Social and Family Development  (MSF) National Council of Social Service  (NCSS) NeuGen Fund Singapore After-Care Association   (SACA) Singapore Anti-Narcotics Association  (SANA) Singapore Prison Service  (SPS) Yellow Ribbon Fund  (YRF) Yellow Ribbon Singapore  (YRSG) Since the inception of the Yellow Ribbon Project, events and activities, such as the Yellow Ribbon Prison Run (which has been renamed to Yellow Ribbon Race in 2022), Yellow Ribbon Song Writing Competition, Yellow Ribbon Community Art & Poetry Exhibition and Yellow Ribbon Culinary Competition, have been organised with the support of community partners to showcase the skills and talents of inmates and ex-offenders.
ANSWER: The Yellow Ribbon Project plays a key role in Singapore's rehabilitation efforts by promoting awareness and acceptance of ex-offenders in the community, supporting their reintegration through community engagement, and showcasing the skills and talents of inmates and ex-offenders through various events and activities. It fosters community action and collaboration among multiple organizations to provide second chances and reduce recidivism. By encouraging social inclusion and rehabilitation, the project helps ex-offenders successfully reintegrate into society and contribute positively to the community.

QUESTION: What are some key international engagements of the Singapore Prison Service (SPS)?
CONTEXT: International Conferences Singapore Prison Service (SPS) has regular engagements with international counterparts that provide opportunities to share latest correctional capabilities and learn from other correctional jurisdictions. These knowledge exchanges enable SPS to sharpen our capabilities for better correctional outcomes and organisational results. SPS participates actively in various international correctional associations, such as the  Asian and Pacific Conference of Correctional Administrators  and the  International Corrections and Prisons Association . The Asian and Pacific Conference of Correctional Administrators (APCCA) is a forum for government officials responsible for prison or correctional administration in the Asia Pacific region. The International Corrections and Prisons Association (ICPA) is an international association which includes government agencies, academic groups, non-government organisations and private sector service providers. At these two correctional associations, SPS shares its experiences in corrections, spanning across operations, staff training, as well as rehabilitation and reintegration. Since 2002, SPS has served as the APCCA Joint-Secretariat with Hong Kong Correctional Services Department. In 2017, then Commissioner of Prisons, Mr Desmond Chin was elected to sit on the ICPA Board of Directors and stepped down in 2020 upon his retirement from SPS. Commissioner of Prisons, Ms Shie Yong Lee was elected to the Board in October 2020 for a four-year term. Apart from these correctional conferences, SPS is also involved in key events organised by the  United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime  (UNODC). Since 2016, at the invitation of UNODC, SPS has contributed to various Expert Group Meetings and provided inputs for handbooks covering topics from prison management to rehabilitation. In 2018, SPS and UNODC jointly organised a training workshop on correctional rehabilitation for 63 participants from various correctional administrations across 16 countries in Asia Pacific. In 2021, SPS and  Yellow Ribbon Singapore  organised a virtual event at the 64 th  United Nations Commission on Narcotic Drugs, titled â€˜Inspiring Community Action in the Rehabilitation and Reintegration of Offendersâ€™. It was attended by over 125 international and local participants. Asian Prisons Lockdown Challenge SPS initiated the biennial Asian Prisons Lockdown Challenge (APLC), a tactical competition for correctional services in the Asia Pacific region to exchange best practices in the field of operational knowledge and tactical skills. The APLC is the first of its kind in the Asia Pacific region, encompassing competition segments that test participantsâ€™ tactical know-how through a series of mentally and physically challenging scenarios. Concurrent tactical workshops helmed by professional trainers are also conducted during the event to share with participants the latest developments in tactical response within correctional settings. Since its inauguration in 2010, the participation in APLC has grown from three to 10 countries with over 70 participants in 2018. Yellow Ribbon Projectâ€™s International Footprints SPSâ€™ sharing of the  Yellow Ribbon Project  at various international forums has shown the world a tenable working model to alleviate the social stigma faced by ex-offenders and create an ecosystem which is ready to accept them after their release. Several jurisdictions and non-state organisations in countries, such as Fiji and the Czech Republic, have been inspired and adapted their brands of the Yellow Ribbon Project.
ANSWER: The Singapore Prison Service engages in international collaborations through participation in key correctional associations such as the Asian and Pacific Conference of Correctional Administrators and the International Corrections and Prisons Association, where it shares experiences in operations, staff training, rehabilitation, and reintegration. SPS has also contributed to United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime events, including Expert Group Meetings and co-organizing a training workshop on correctional rehabilitation for participants from 16 Asian and Pacific countries. Additionally, SPS initiated the Asian Prisons Lockdown Challenge to exchange tactical skills and best practices, and it has promoted its Yellow Ribbon Project internationally, inspiring similar initiatives in countries like Fiji and the Czech Republic.

QUESTION: What are the eligibility requirements to become a Senior Prison Officer in Singapore?
CONTEXT: Senior Prison Officer Embark on a highly meaningful career in corrections, where you can make a lasting impact on society.  You will be a leader in corrections, trained and empowered to think creatively to shape the future of a safer and more inclusive Singapore. Working in an ethical and dynamic environment, ours is a job that calls for nerves of steel, but a lot of heart. Be fulfilled beyond the ordinary - join us to transform lives. Eligibility Requirements A pass degree in any discipline Medically and physically fit (For Men: PES B1 and above, with at least a â€œpassâ€ in latest IPPT) Normal colour vision Remuneration and Benefits Remuneration and Benefits  Salary $5,070 - $6,200++ *Indicative starting salary for fresh graduates with the completion of full-time National Service.   ++Additional increment may be granted for relevant work experience. Sign-On Bonus Newly appointed Senior Prison Officers will receive a sign-on bonus of $15,000 (given in two tranches of $5,000 upon appointment and $10,000 upon confirmation in service).* 	*Eligibility criteria and conditions apply. Benefits Annual vacation leave of 18 - 21 days  Annual medical leave of 14 days or 60 days with hospitalisation Annual bonus and performance bonus  Medical and dental subsidies Sponsorship for continuous education  Group insurance  Staff welfare benefits Use of government holiday chalets  INVEST Retirement Benefits A contribution equivalent to 13.25% of your gross salary will be credited monthly into your retirement account, starting from your 5th year in service and will cease when you reach 56 years old. The monies are invested to earn investment returns. Upon retirement, you can withdraw the full sum in your account. Strategic Payment Scheme You can look forward to cash payouts of 3 months quantum of your gross salary (ASP and below) or 2.5 months quantum of your gross salary (DSP and above) upon reaching your 3rd, 6th, 9th, 12th, 15th, 20th, and 25thÂ year in service. Training As a new entrant officer, you will be required to undergo a 38-week Senior Prison Officer  Course which comprises a residential phase at the Home Team Academy and on-the-job training at one of the prison institutions. Â  You will also undergo regular in-service training, relevant functional training and courses, and milestone leadership development programs for your professional and personal growth as you advance in your career in Corrections.  Career Development and Upgrading Opportunities Career Development SPS provides a dynamic and structured career roadmap to groom you as a leader in Corrections. You can look forward to a wide range of posting opportunities and will undergo the relevant development and training programs. Foundational Posting After graduating from the Senior Prison Officer Course, you will be deployed as a Senior Correctional Unit Officer (SCUO) in one of the prison institutions. Developmental Posting After your foundational posting, you may be deployed to one of the HQ staff divisions where you will hone and develop your staff work skills. Read about Zhi Mianâ€™s career journey as a Captain of Lives  here (https://www.scholarschoice.com.sg/experience/helping-inmates-break-the-cycle-of-crime/)
ANSWER: To become a Senior Prison Officer in Singapore, you must have a pass degree in any discipline, be medically and physically fit (for men, with a PES B1 and above and a pass in the latest IPPT), and have normal colour vision.

QUESTION: What roles can volunteers play in supporting the rehabilitation of inmates and ex-offenders within the Singapore Prison Service?
CONTEXT:
Volunteers work with the inmates and ex-offenders in the areas of religious programmes and services, social programmes, academic and enrichment programmes. Volunteers are also one of the sources of pro-social support for ex-offenders in the community. The Roles of a Volunteers in an Offenderâ€™s Rehabilitation Journey As a volunteer, you play an integral role in the inmateâ€™s rehabilitation and desistance journey. When you engage inmates, ex-offenders and their families, you give hope and help rebuild lives. As a volunteer, you: Share knowledge and impart skills to help inmates increase their reintegration potential. Provide the emotional support that encourages inmates to stay focused and committed to their rehabilitation while serving sentence. Assist to stabilise and strengthen families left vulnerable when a loved one is incarcerated. Form part of the pro-social network in the community, to reinforce the ex-offendersâ€™ resolve to desist and support the reunification of families after release. The path to recovery and desistance can be challenging and is rarely straightforward. The Singapore Prison Service welcomes you to join us on our journey towards a society without re-offending. Areas where you can Contribute as a Volunteer The programmes and services facilitated by volunteers complement the  rehabilitative initiatives  provided by SPS to better prepare offenders for eventual return to the society. Volunteering can be in various forms, within and beyond the prison walls. You can volunteer to: Be an SPS Befriender Pro-social support is among the key factors that prevent re-offending. You can volunteer to be an SPS Befriender and be a friend to inmates who want to restart their lives among positive peers. As a Befriender, you will support inmates who may be at any phase of their incarceration through letter-writing, phonecalls and visitations. The befriending journey continues into the aftercare as your befriendee prepares to re-enter society with your support. Be an Academic Tutor Academic education is one of the rehabilitation programmes available to inmates. Together with full-time teachers from Ministry of Education and independent adjunct teachers, you support the inmatesâ€™ aspirations towards academic qualifications. Be a Facilitator of Religious, Family, or other Personal Development Programmes Volunteers facilitating religious programmes and services provide spiritual guidance and emotional support to inmates. Â  When volunteers facilitate family programmes or services, you help inmates and their families gain useful knowledge and skills to become more effective members of the family. With your support they will gain stability and strength to move forward as a family, beyond their release. Children also benefit both directly and indirectly when families are supported by volunteers. Â  Volunteers possess a wealth of experience and competencies. You can volunteer to impart skills and share knowledge, such as IT literacy and financial planning, with the inmates and their families.        Be a Yellow Ribbon Community Project (YRCP) volunteer YRCP is a grassroots-led initiative that provides community support and assistance to families impacted by incarceration through grassroots organisations. As a YRCP volunteer, you will engage and befriend these families and where necessary, link them up with helping agencies in the community. Be a Volunteer Case Officer (VCO) As a VCO, you will support community corrections work in the areas of case management and provision of prosocial support for supervisees who are emplaced on community supervision programmes to help steer, coach, and guide them to live a prosocial life. You will also help expand the superviseesâ€™ network of support in the community. Â  Â For more information on the VCO scheme, please check out the  VCO FAQ PDF link . Click here  to join the Singapore Prison Service as a volunteer.

FAQ for Volunteer Case Officers (VCOs)    1. Can I sign up as a VCO if I am currently working in a full-time job?  Yes. As long as you are able to engage your assigned supervisees at least once a month,  support them in their reintegration journey and provide monthly progress reports, you are  welcomed to apply.    2. Do I get any form of renumeration as a VCO?  VCOs serve as volunteers without renumeration. However, to acknowledge the effort and  recognise that some expenses may be incurred, an honorarium will be provided.    3. What if I am not able to engage the supervisee and provide the progress report for the  month?  Please inform your assigned SPS staff if engagements were not possible because of an  unresponsive supervisee. Please inform the VCO team if engagements were not possible  due to personal reasons.     4. Do I have to go into Changi Prison Complex or Selarang Park Complex to engage  supervisees?  For the first session, we strongly encourage all VCOs to have a joint session together with  the supervisee and SPS staff. The venue will depend on whether your supervisee is still  incarcerated or undergoing a Community-based Programme. Subsequently, VCOs can  engage supervisees independently at designated locations within the community.    5. Do I have to engage the supervisees in-person?  This is strongly encouraged, especially for the first few sessions to build rapport. After  establishing rapport, the VCO and supervisee can decide on the mode of engagement  that best suits them (e.g., in person, video call, etc.).    6. Will clinical supervision be provided?  No. SPS does not provide clinical supervision for VCOs. If you are currently a student,  please approach the clinical supervisors from your school. Alternatively, you may wish to  engage an external clinical supervisor for support. Please note that your clinical  supervisor has to be registered with a professional body (e.g., the Singapore Association  of Counselling) and sign an Official Secrets Act (OSA) form with SPS.    7. Can I request for more, or less, cases?  Yes. You may inform the VCO team of your preference. However, case assignment  depends on several factors, such as availability of cases, matching of profiles, etc. We  encourage all VCOs to have at least one active case at any time.    8. What if I cannot take up any cases for a specific period of time?  Please let the VCO team know of your situation.
ANSWER: Volunteers can support the rehabilitation of inmates and ex-offenders by acting as SPS Befrienders, providing emotional support through letter-writing, phone calls, and visitations, and continuing support during aftercare. They can also serve as Academic Tutors, helping inmates pursue academic qualifications, or as Facilitators of religious, family, or personal development programmes, offering spiritual guidance and family support. Additionally, volunteers can contribute as Yellow Ribbon Community Project (YRCP) members to assist families affected by incarceration, or as Volunteer Case Officers (VCOs) to provide prosocial support and case management for ex-offenders on community supervision.

QUESTION: What training and development programs are provided to new senior prison officers in the Singapore Prison Service?
CONTEXT: As a new entrant officer, you will be required to undergo a 38-week Senior Prison Officer  Course which comprises a residential phase at the Home Team Academy and on-the-job training at one of the prison institutions. The course will equip you with the relevant knowledge and skills to perform your duties. All new Captains of Lives are required to serve a two-year bond after graduating from the course. You will also undergo regular in-service training, relevant functional training and courses, and milestone leadership development programs for your professional and personal growth as you advance in your career in Corrections.
ANSWER: New senior prison officers in the Singapore Prison Service undergo a 38-week Senior Prison Officer Course that includes a residential phase at the Home Team Academy and on-the-job training at a prison institution. They also participate in regular in-service training, relevant functional training, and milestone leadership development programs to support their professional and personal growth throughout their careers. Additionally, all new Captains of Lives are required to serve a two-year bond after completing the course.

QUESTION: What career development opportunities are available for Senior Prison Officers in the Singapore Prison Service?
CONTEXT: Career Development SPS provides a dynamic and structured career roadmap to groom you as a leader in Corrections. You can look forward to a wide range of posting opportunities and will undergo the relevant development and training programs. Foundational Posting After graduating from the Senior Prison Officer Course, you will be deployed as a Senior Correctional Unit Officer (SCUO) in one of the prison institutions. As a SCUO, you will ensure the effective and efficient running of the operations and rehabilitation programmes in the correctional unit. You will lead a team of like minded and highly skilled Captains of Lives to achieve the goals of safe and secure custody of inmates, as well as their successful rehabilitation and reintegration into society. Through purposeful interactions with offenders, you will guide and steer them towards a life without offending. Developmental Posting After your foundational posting, you may be deployed to one of the HQ staff divisions where you will hone and develop your staff work skills. Through regular job rotations across operational and staff positions, you will grow in your leadership and organisational management skills as a leader. If you are found suitable, there will also be opportunities for secondment to Ministry of Home Affairs and relevant external agencies for further exposure and development. Upgrading Opportunities SPS supports the professional and personal growth of our Captains of Lives through various upgrading schemes. MHA Scholarship for Full-time Postgraduate Studies (by nomination only) MHA Sponsorship/MHA Study Award for part-time studies* Professional Development Leave (up to 10 weeks paid leave) * Learning & Development Subsidy Scheme ($700 per annum) Enhanced Learning & Development Subsidy Scheme ($10,000 in the last five years prior to retirement) * All permanent and confirmed officers are eligible to apply, subject to the criteria and approval.
ANSWER: Senior Prison Officers in the Singapore Prison Service have access to a structured career roadmap that includes foundational posting as a Senior Correctional Unit Officer (SCUO), where they lead teams in managing prison operations and inmate rehabilitation. They can then advance to developmental postings in HQ staff divisions, gaining experience through job rotations and leadership training, with potential for secondment to the Ministry of Home Affairs or other external agencies. Additionally, they can pursue upgrading opportunities such as the MHA Scholarship for full-time postgraduate studies, MHA Sponsorship for part-time studies, Professional Development Leave, and enhanced learning subsidies to support their professional and personal growth.

QUESTION: How can someone support inmates in their rehabilitation and re-entry into society through the Singapore Prison Service?
CONTEXT: Pro-social support is among the key factors that prevent re-offending. You can volunteer to be an SPS Befriender and be a friend to inmates who want to restart their lives among positive peers. As a Befriender, you will support inmates who may be at any phase of their incarceration through letter-writing, phonecalls and visitations. The befriending journey continues into the aftercare as your befriendee prepares to re-enter society with your support.
ANSWER: Someone can support inmates in their rehabilitation and re-entry into society by volunteering as an SPS Befriender, offering ongoing support through letter-writing, phone calls, and visitations during incarceration. This support helps inmates at any stage of their journey by fostering positive relationships with peers. The befriending relationship continues into aftercare, assisting inmates as they prepare to re-enter society.

QUESTION: What role does academic education play in the rehabilitation of inmates?

ANSWER: Academic education plays a key role in the rehabilitation of inmates by helping them pursue academic qualifications and fulfill their aspirations. QUESTION: How can SPS volunteers support inmates and their families?
CONTEXT: Volunteers facilitating religious programmes and services provide spiritual guidance and emotional support to inmates. When volunteers facilitate family programmes or services, you help inmates and their families gain useful knowledge and skills to become more effective members of the family. With your support they will gain stability and strength to move forward as a family, beyond their release. Children also benefit both directly and indirectly when families are supported by volunteers. Volunteers possess a wealth of experience and competencies. You can volunteer to impart skills and share knowledge, such as IT literacy and financial planning, with the inmates and their families.
ANSWER: SPS volunteers support inmates and their families by providing spiritual guidance and emotional support through religious programmes and services. They help inmates and their families gain valuable skills and knowledge, such as IT literacy and financial planning, which enhance family stability and strength. By facilitating family programmes, volunteers enable families to become more effective and resilient, supporting long-term success beyond incarceration.

QUESTION: What is the role of a YRCP volunteer in supporting families impacted by incarceration?
CONTEXT: YRCP is a grassroots-led initiative that provides community support and assistance to families impacted by incarceration through grassroots organisations. As a YRCP volunteer, you will engage and befriend these families and where necessary, link them up with helping agencies in the community.
ANSWER: A YRCP volunteer engages and befriends families impacted by incarceration, providing emotional support and building trust within the community. They also help connect these families with relevant local agencies and resources to meet their needs. Through grassroots collaboration, volunteers assist in creating a supportive network that addresses the challenges faced by these families.

They help steer, coach, and guide supervisees toward living a prosocial life and assist in expanding their network of community support.

QUESTION: How does Singapore Prison Service ensure rehabilitation and reintegration?
CONTEXT: SPS Rehabilitation and Reintegration Approach The SPS Rehabilitation and Reintegration approach guides SPS and community partnersâ€™ collective rehabilitation and reintegration efforts. Â     SPS adopts a seamless throughcare approach where there are continuous and coordinated efforts to address the risks and needs of inmates while they are inside prison, which is followed through in the community upon their release. It is multi-faceted and considers how inmates as individuals interact with their   eco-system. Â     The journey involves many different levels of agents of change, such as Inmates Captains of Lives Family members Employers Volunteers Community partners Desistors Throughcare Approach The rehabilitation and reintegration journey for offenders can be divided into two key phases:  -  Incare  (in prison) and   -  Aftercare  (in the community). Â  Responsible and Motivated Individuals    At the core of any change journey is the individual themselves â€“ individuals must accept responsibility and be motivated to  initiate and sustain change  in their lives. By cultivating a transformative environment in prison, SPS encourages inmates to take  ownership  of their lives and develop a pro-social identity. Â     At SPS, we prepare inmates for their reintegration as active members of society through various programmes. Keeping the dierent needs of each individual in mind, we conduct a range of correctional programmes: psychology-based correctional programmes, family programmes, work programmes, vocational training and education.  Risk Needs Responsivity Model, Strength-based and Desistance Theories Singapore Prison Serviceâ€™s rehabilitation approach is informed by various established models, including Risk Needs Responsivity (RNR) and desistance theories, seeking to address the inmateâ€™s offending risks, rehabilitation and reintegration needs, leveraging on social and community networks, to prevent re-offending.      Psychology-Based Correctional Programmes Inmates can attend  Psychology-Based Programmes , enhancing their motivation to address and change their criminal thinking patterns.      My Action Plan Â Â  Â Â Â Â  Â Â Â Â  Â Â Â Â  Â Â Â Â  My Action Plan  is a planning tool that helps inmates chart their goals towards rehabilitation, encouraging them to take greater ownership of their own journey.Â  Â Â Â Â  Â Â  Â  Vocational and Skills Training Â Â  Â Â Â Â  SPS partners with  Yellow Ribbon Singapore  to improve inmatesâ€™ capabilities through skills training and provide career opportunities for them to reintegrate into society. Â  	 Â Â Â Â  Empowering Interpersonal Networks    Forming new and healthy social networks is vital for inmates in their reintegration journey. SPS supports them through creating opportunities for them to  strengthen familial bonds, develop prosocial networks and prevent intergenerational offending. Â     Within the prison, many agents of change play a role in the development of prosocial relations, including the inmates themselves, Captains of Lives (COLs), community partners and volunteers. Outside the prison, these reintegration efforts are supported by family members,  our community partners , and  volunteers  who are inmatesâ€™ motivation to stay on the right path.   COLs as Role Models Captains of Lives (COLs)  are often the first agent of change for inmates. They embody SPSâ€™s mission and core values in their daily interactions with the inmates to instil discipline, meaningfully engage them, and inspire change.      Family members as reasons to change SPS recognises that family support is important in the inmatesâ€™ rehabilitation and reintegration journey. Â  SPS involves the family members of inmates as key agents of change through  family-centric rehabilitation programmes ,  letter correspondences , and  visits . This helps inmates prepare for their reintegration so they can return to a supportive family network upon release.      Volunteers who expand prosocial networks Volunteers  are crucial community partners who inspire change in inmates and expand their prosocial networks. They complement SPSâ€™s rehabilitation efforts through structured religious and secular programmes and offer reintegration support upon inmatesâ€™ release.      Dedicated Communities To reduce long-term re-offending rates, a strong support system within the community is essential. This involves collaboration between community partners, employers, families, and government agencies to promote desistance. Â     At SPS, we encourage community involvement by working with community partners and volunteers to other structured programmes to support inmatesâ€™ reintegration. Our efforts also extend to their families, who play an integral role in the rehabilitation journey. Tailored support is also provided by specific communities to provide more targeted intervention for inmates.   Community-Based Programmes (CBPs) and Halfway Houses CBPs  promote the reintegration of inmates by allowing suitable inmates to serve the tail-end of their sentence/detention in the community. Â  Under the Halfway House Service Model, SPS partners 9 halfway houses to support the reintegration of supervisees into the community.      Desistor Network Desistor Network aims to create a strong network where agencies who work with and support desistors can tap on one anotherâ€™s strengths, experience, and resources to expand the range of support activities available to desistors and ex-offenders.      Throughcare Volunteer Framework (TVF) TVF volunteers engage with inmates throughout the entire rehabilitation journey, both Incare and Aftercare, providing a more pro-social support network upon their release.      Yellow Ribbon Community Project (YRCP) This grassroots-led project provides support and assistance to inmates and their families, allowing them to focus on reintegrating back into society. Â  SPS also engages Family Service Centres (FSCs) to perform family case management to support inmatesâ€™ families with community resources.      An Inclusive Nation SPS and Yellow Ribbon Singapore, working as part of the Community Action for the Rehabilitation of Ex-Offenders (CARE) Network, aim to influence societal changes to shape an inclusive society that accepts and support ex-offenders and their families. Â     We aim to expand awareness of rehabilitative efforts and build up public trust and acceptance of ex-offenders reintegrating into society. To do so, we partner with the public, employers, schools, community partners and government agencies to support ex-offenders in the reintegration process, improving their social mobility as they work towards becoming contributing members of society. CARE Network CARE Network (CN)  brings together community partners and government organisations to support the smooth transition from incare to aftercare, providing ex-offenders and their families the support they need after release. Â  As part of CN, there are dedicated charitable funds such as Yellow Ribbon Fund and NeuGen Fund that support the development an implementation of programmes and services for inmates, ex-offenders, and their families.      Employers YRSG partners with over 6,200 companies to give ex-offenders a second chance to rebuild their careers. To make this possible, YRSG matches ex-offenders to suitable job openings and equips them with necessary skills through training, career guidance and job retention support.      Yellow Ribbon Project The Yellow Ribbon Project highlights the importance of giving ex-offenders a second chance and welcoming them back into society. It promotes the 3 â€˜Aâ€™s needed to improve support for ex-offenders: raising  Awareness , generating  Acceptance  and inspiring community  Action .
ANSWER: The Singapore Prison Service ensures rehabilitation and reintegration through a seamless throughcare approach that spans both prison and community phases, guided by the Risk Needs Responsivity and desistance theories. It offers tailored programs including psychology-based initiatives, vocational training, family involvement, and work opportunities, while empowering inmates through tools like the My Action Plan and Captains of Lives to take ownership of their transformation. Community support is strengthened via partnerships with halfway houses, volunteers, employers, and the CARE Network, which promotes acceptance, awareness, and action to help ex-offenders reintegrate successfully into society.

QUESTION: What models and approaches guide the psychology-based correctional programmes offered by the Singapore Prison Service?
CONTEXT: Singapore Prison Service provides a range of rehabilitation programmes for inmates under our care. These programmes are psychology-based and aims to: Motivate inmates to change and Work on their negative thinking patterns that led to their offending. These programmes also provide opportunities for inmates to practice and enhance their ability to communicate with others and manage their emotions, so that they can work towards a life free from drugs and crime. The development of our psychology-based correctional programmes (PCPs) are guided by: Research on the needs of our inmates Established rehabilitation frameworks Best practices from overseas correctional programmes Specifically, our programmes are guided by the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model, the Good Lives Model (GLM) and the Desistance Approach. For specific populations such as women and youth, relevant intervention strategies are incorporated in the programmes to meet their unique needs. General Offending Programmes Develop skills to solve problems Develop plans to achieve their goals and values to desist from crime Identify and dispute unhelpful thinking patterns that led to their crimes Drug Offending Programmes Develop skills to manage their emotions Develop plans to manage their slips and relapses Form new pro-social habits, in replacement of their habit of using drugs Identify and dispute unhelpful thinking patterns that led to their abuse of drugs Youth Offending Programmes Build skills to manage conflicts constructively Develop skills to resist anti-social peer influence Explore their values and goals to achieve healthy self-development Identify and dispute unhelpful thinking patterns that led to their misconduct Gender-Responsive Programmes Build skills to manage their unhelpful emotions Develop their pro-social identity by encouraging and affirming them Build healthy relationships with significant others to develop their support network Identify and dispute unhelpful thinking patterns that led to their offending behaviours Violent Offending Programmes Identify and dispute unhelpful thinking patterns that led them to violence Develop skills to manage their emotions Develop pro-social conflict resolution and communication skills Develop relapse prevention plans for their violent offending Sexual Offending Programmes Address issues related to self-esteem, shame and guilt Identify and dispute unhelpful thinking patterns that led them to sexual offending Develop healthy emotional and sexual self-regulation skills Improve problem-solving, interpersonal and relational skills Increase empathy towards victims and others Develop relapse prevention plans for their sexual offending
ANSWER: The psychology-based correctional programmes offered by the Singapore Prison Service are guided by the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model, the Good Lives Model (GLM), and the Desistance Approach. These frameworks are supplemented by research on inmate needs, established rehabilitation principles, and best practices from overseas correctional systems. Additionally, specific programmes for women, youth, and other populations incorporate gender-responsive and age-appropriate intervention strategies to meet their unique needs.

QUESTION: What is the purpose of the CARE Network and how does it support ex-offenders?
CONTEXT: Thousands of inmates are released from prison each year. Their transition back to the community is fraught with the difficulties of readjustment and accentuated by stigmatisation of their past imprisonment. With this in mind, the CARE Network was set up in 2000 with seven founding agencies â€“ MHA, SPS, YRSG, NCSS, SACA, SANA, and ISCOS â€“ to provide an inclusive and supportive environment for ex-offendersâ€™ reintegration. Subsequently, MSF (then Ministry of Community Development, Youth and Sports) joined the Network in 2005 and YRF in 2015 as core members. Over the years, the Network has also expanded to work with over 100 community partners, including SSAs, religious groups, Family Service Centres (FSCs), Halfway Houses (HWHs), grassroots organisations, corporates and schools. As part of community engagement efforts, CARE Network launched two key initiatives in 2004 â€“ the Yellow Ribbon Project (YRP) and the Yellow Ribbon Fund (YRF). YRP is a national public engagement campaign aimed at changing societyâ€™s mindset in giving ex-offenders a second chance in life. YRF is the first charitable fund dedicated to providing support for reintegration programmes/services for inmates, ex-offenders and their families. Beyond community engagement, the CARE Network hopes to empower and promote shared ownership among partners. Effective linkages with the community would help provide a holistic and sustainable support network for ex-offenders and their families. Our Initiatives Advancing Aftercare Capabilities and Capacity As part of CARE Network's effort to build evidence - based practices in the aftercare sector,Â  research Â was undertaken by SACA, which took reference from existing local and overseas studies. Â  After months of consultation with aftercare professionals, theÂ  Aftercare Contextualised Competency Framework (ACF) Â framework was launch in 2021. The framework was adapted from the Skills Framework for Social Service (Social Work) and contextualised to the aftercare sector by outlining key competencies, skills and knowledge areas distinct to Aftercare related work. You can access the framework  here (https://file.go.gov.sg/aftercarecompetencyframework.pdf) . The same year, the first  CARE Network training course "Corrections and Aftercare Landscape "  was held in mid-May. The course was jointly developed by SPS and SACA to provide contextual information of Singapore's corrections and aftercare landscape and a total of 51 aftercare professionals attended. Targeted Support for Clients The Case Management Framework (CMF)  was implemented in SACA and SANA in 2001. It is a holistic approach in counselling, comprising both incare and aftercare components, to enable higher levels of functioning and greater resilience in clients as they prepare for their eventual re-entry. In 2015, to better manage the complex needs of offenders in their rehabilitation journey, the  Developmental Framework for Offender Rehab Personnel (DORP)  was implemented to ensure professionalism and enhance service delivery. Â  Support for Education and Skills Upgrading Â  The launch of  Lee Foundation Education Assistance Scheme (LFEAS)  in 2003 provides clients with the opportunity to pursue GCE ('N', 'O' and 'A') Levels, and tertiary qualifications through a co-payment scheme. In 2010, the  YRF STAR (Skills Training Assistance to Restart) Bursary   and  ISCOS' Skills Assistance Subsidy Scheme (SASS)  were launched to enable upgrading and upskilling.  Community Engagements and Collective Action To expand its reach in the community, CARE Network launched the YRP and YRF in 2004, and the Yellow Ribbon Community Project (YRCP) in 2010 to reach out to families. In 2015, the Network welcomed YRF as a core member to ensure CARE Networkâ€™s objectives are better resourced, and to align YRFâ€™s funding with national priorities to maximise impact. 			  Â  In 2019, ahead of the Networkâ€™s 20th Anniversary celebrations in 2020, a comprehensiveÂ  review of CARE Network Â was initiated to rally collective effort to reduce the 2-year and 5-year recidivism rates and chart the future direction of the Network. Over 70 partner agencies and 350 individuals were engaged. Three working groups led by core members (SACA, SANA, ISCOS and SPS) were formed to drive the review. The recommendations were consolidated into two strategic thrusts, namely: (i) advancing CARE Network capabilities and capacity, and (ii) strengthening throughcare and reintegration journey. AÂ  Digital Publication Â was launched in 2020, as CARE Network celebrates its 20th Anniversary, to align collective efforts with the strategic thrusts. 	 Â  CARE Network Events Â  The first CARE Network Seminar was held in 2013. Since then, the annual event has
ANSWER: The purpose of the CARE Network is to provide an inclusive and supportive environment that facilitates the reintegration of ex-offenders into society by addressing the challenges of readjustment and societal stigma. It supports ex-offenders through various initiatives such as the Yellow Ribbon Project and Yellow Ribbon Fund, which promote public awareness and provide financial support for reintegration programs, as well as through structured frameworks like the Aftercare Contextualised Competency Framework and Case Management Framework that enhance professional capabilities and holistic client care.

Held on 18 Jan, the production was graced by President Halimah Yacob as the guest-of-honour and paid tribute to the unsung heroes who made second chances possible. ANSWER: The key learning outcomes of the Cognitive Behaviour Therapy training offered by the Social Service Institute include constructing CBT case conceptualizations for specific case scenarios, using behavioural change strategies to sustain client change, applying attachment styles to address client needs, and preparing CBT treatment plans tailored to clients' specific requirements. ANSWER: Professionals play a crucial role in supporting individuals with mental illness during their recovery by identifying safety issues, explaining the challenges they face, and applying trauma-informed and recovery-focused approaches. They help clients develop personalized recovery action plans, use motivational interviewing techniques to encourage positive change, and equip individuals with skills to manage symptoms and improve emotional regulation. QUESTION: What is the purpose of the CARE Network established by the Singapore Prison Service?
CONTEXT: lower  the re-offending rate, help  more ex-offenders to desist  from crime and drugs, and  strengthen more families. With this strong sense of  mission and close  collaborations among our  partners, we are confident  that we will be able to bring  about a breakthrough that  will make our society a safer  place for everyone. Without our partners,  CARE Network will  simply not be where we  are today. It is through our shared  vision, passion and  perseverance of every  individual that have  allowed us to achieve  this milestone. But there will always be  new challenges, and only  by working together can  we overcome them. Works are in the pipeline  to provide platforms for  more inter-agency  partnerships and  collaborations to take  place. It is through  collective action that we  can provide the greatest impact for our clients. As we approach the end  of the year, it is timely for  us to affirm one another,  for rising above the  challenges that were  thrown our way. A grateful and sincere  THANK YOU to all our  members and partners  for your incredible effort.  Let us press on and  emerge even stronger. The Community Action for the Rehabilitation of Ex-Offenders  (CARE) Network was formed 20 years ago in May 2000. We are a coalition of like-minded agencies, motivated by a common  purpose, to help ex-offenders transit back into the community as  contributing citizens. The achievements and transformations in the  aftercare sector over the last two decades have been nothing short  of momentous, made possible through the hard work and dogged  dedication of all our partners and volunteers. This year, as we commemorate our 20th anniversary, we are also  assailed by the COVID-19 pandemic which has been described as a  once in a lifetime crisis that severely upended the well-being and  security of many people, with potentially long-lasting consequences.  Originally slated to be a special year of celebrations, we  have now redirected our efforts to bring more Hope,  Confidence, and Opportunities to ex-offenders and their  families in these trying times. The  Next Bound A Network of  Opportunities Our Heartfelt Thanks 03 FOREWORD Shie Yong Lee Commissioner of Prisons Co-Chairman of CARE Network Chng Hwee Hong Chairman of  Yellow Ribbon Singapore Co-Chairman of CARE Network CARE Network: A Pictorial History, 2000-2020 CARE Network celebrates two decades of achievements, made possible by our collective effort as a  united network. Here, we honour those who have paved the way for others to join in the pursuit of  providing seamless throughcare support for ex-offenders. Explore the pictorial journey below as we  celebrate our milestones - from past to present. Happy Birthday! CARE Network was  born with a vision to offer Hope,  Confidence  and  Opportunities for ex-offenders. CARE Network was launched officially  on 29 January 2002, along with the  unveiling of our own CARE Network  logo - a double celebration! Selected  from over 500 inmatesâ€™ entries, our  logo  embodies  the  concept  of  â€œturning over a new leafâ€.  The Case Management Framework  (CMF) was implemented in SACA  and SANA as a holistic approach to  assist  ex-offenders  in  their  reintegration needs. 2000 2001 To  garner  greater  community  support, CARE Network launched  the Yellow Ribbon Project (YRP)  and Yellow Ribbon Fund (YRF).  These initiatives continue to be the  cornerstone for our cause. 2004 2002 OUR JOURNEY THUS FAR: Achievements and Milestones 04 The Attachment Programme and  Learning Journeys were introduced  to build capabilities and capacity  for the aftercare sector. 2011 Ministry of Social and Family  Development (MSF), then Ministry  of  Community  Development,  Youth and Sports (MCYS) joined  CARE Network as one of our  core members. 2005 2009 2010 2013 Many new initiatives were launched  to build a vibrant aftercare sector.  The Yellow Ribbon Community  Project (YRCP) was launched to  reach out to families. The Yellow  Ribbon Fund STAR Bursary and  ISCOS Skills Assistance Subsidy  Scheme were launched to enable  upgrading and upskilling. The  inaugural  CARE  Network  Workplan Seminar was launched as  a platform for knowledge sharing  and networking among partners.  The  inaugural  Yellow  Ribbon  Prison Run (YRPR) was held. YRPR  has since been a signature YRP  event to engage the public in  building a society that supports  second chances.  OUR JOURNEY THUS FAR: Achievements and Milestones
ANSWER: The purpose of the CARE Network, established by the Singapore Prison Service, is to help ex-offenders transition back into society as contributing citizens by providing seamless aftercare support, reducing re-offending rates, and strengthening families through collaborative efforts among agencies, volunteers, and community partners. It was formed in 2000 with a vision to offer hope, confidence, and opportunities for ex-offenders, and has since grown into a united coalition dedicated to rehabilitation and reintegration. The network achieves this through initiatives like the Yellow Ribbon Project, Case Management Framework, and various programs that support skill development, family engagement, and community inclusion.

ANSWER: To improve the aftercare sector for ex-offenders, CARE Network launched a comprehensive review in early 2019 that engaged over 70 agencies and 350 individuals through one-to-one interviews, focus group discussions, and a retreat. Three review workgroups were formed to focus on professionalising the aftercare sector, enhancing reintegration through community collaboration, and improving throughcare, with recommendations aimed at amending the Misuse of Drugs Act, increasing community corrections placements, and advancing digitalisation in line with the SMART Nation initiative. These efforts are expected to lead to new initiatives and a stronger, more effective aftercare system for ex-offenders.

QUESTION: What are the strategic plans of the CARE Network to reduce recidivism among ex-offenders?
CONTEXT: Rockey Francisco Junior  SPS  RWG3 Lead The CARE Network RWG3 provided an opportunity for SPS and  YRSG (then SCORE) to work more closely with CARE Network  partners such as ISCOS, SANA and SACA but also with CNB,  Fitrah, SINDA and HWHs like Jamiyah and Ashram. Discussions  threw up fresh ideas as well as ways to improve current  processes. We hope that our proposals would contribute to  enhancing throughcare and improving the 5-year recidivism rate. 09 After nine months of extensive consultations with our stakeholders, CARE Network has arrived at two  strategic thrusts, with the overarching goal of reducing the recidivism rate. The two-pronged approach  seeks to optimise human capital and enhance institutional strengths in the sector, and to address the  complex needs of ex-offenders through enabling a continuum of care. Key plans include developing a contextualized aftercare competency framework and two new introductory training courses for caseworkers, expanding accessible e-training for volunteers, launching a digitally enabled network with a shared resource depository and referral service, forming a Reintegration Committee with self-help groups and desistors, and supporting pro-social support groups to empower ex-offenders in their reintegration and community contribution.

ANSWER: The purpose of the CARE Network is to support the reintegration of ex-offenders by fostering community involvement and collaboration through a collective effort that empowers ex-offenders and their families. It aims to build a safer, more inclusive society by providing tailored support, expanding partnerships across various sectors, and creating a sustainable network of resources to assist ex-offenders throughout their rehabilitation journey. The network also seeks to professionalize aftercare services through a competency framework and to engage diverse stakeholders in helping ex-offenders transition successfully back into society.

QUESTION: What is the mission of the Singapore Prison Service (SPS)?
CONTEXT: Ministry of Home Affairs Ministry of Home AffairsÂ  MHA consists of MHA Headquarters, 7 Home Team departments and 3 statutory boards, and is known collectively as the Home Team. We work as One Home Team, in close partnership with the community, to keep Singapore safe and secure. Each Home Team Department has its specific roles and responsibilities, working towards a common mission and vision. Our Home Team officers work tirelessly to keep Singapore safe and secure amidst an increasingly complex operating landscape. The Home Team is constantly innovating to ensure we are always ready and able to respond effectively, and to serve the public better. MSF develops the â€œheartwareâ€ for Singapore through its policies, community infrastructure, programmes and services. QUESTION: What role do Case Management Partners (CMPs) play in the rehabilitation of offenders and support for their families?
CONTEXT: Strong community support, at each stage of the offenderâ€™s rehabilitation journey, has and will remain key in helping offenders live crime-free and productive lives, and help to build strong family units. Today, many from the community have joined Singapore Prison Service (SPS) on our journey towards a society without re-offending. These are volunteers partnering SPS to provide programmes and initiatives to offenders, ex-offenders and their families. This strong and growing partnership with a healthy cross-section of the community is critical for the successful rehabilitation and reintegration of offenders. Case Management Partners SPS works closely with Case Management Partners (CMPs) to provide case management services and support for inmates and their families through (a) needs identification, (b) individualised service planning and (c) implementation of intervention and measures to address those needs. By integrating a range of services in the community, CMPs are able to address the respective domains of needs such as employment, accommodation, financial issues and coping skills. Â  From left to right:  Association of Muslim Professionals (AMP) ,  Singapore Anti-Narcotics Association (SANA) ,  Singapore After-Care Association (SACA) ,  Fei Yue Community Services ,  Industrial & Services Co-operative Society (ISCOS) ,  Youth Guidance Outreach Services (YGOS) ,  REACH Community Services Â  In addition to case management, CMPs also offer other services that help with the inmatesâ€™ reintegration into society. For example, our longstanding partners, SANA and SACA, offer voluntary walk-in services for inmates who require additional aftercare support in the form of the Step-Up Centre and Good Samaritans Reintegration Assistance Scheme respectively. Such initiatives are also extended to the families of inmates. 			 Halfway Houses Community partners such as the Halfway Houses continue to partner SPS under the Halfway House Service Model. CARE Network Click to enlarge Faith-based volunteers In addition to providing a strong religious foundation, which can be important for individuals to steer away from a life of crime, these programmes incorporate moral development and offer emotional support for inmates to cope with lifeâ€™s experiences and help them better prepare for their release. Â  As part of the Throughcare Volunteer Framework, our religious volunteers also continue to provide pro-social support to offenders when they are released. Yellow Ribbon Singapore Yellow Ribbon Singapore or YRSG, is a statutory board under the Ministry of Home Affairs. SPS partners closely with YRSG to provide skills training and employment assistance for offenders and ex-offenders, and to engage the community through the Yellow Ribbon Project. Career Coaches Career coaches conduct assessments, job profiling and matching of offenders with suitable jobs. They also provide career coaching to help offenders understand their employment pathways and address potential work challenges. Suitable CBP supervisees may be assigned to career coaches to assist them in their employment matters.  Secular Volunteers To complement SPSâ€™s efforts in rehabilitating inmates, we also partner with more than 1,300 volunteers from more than 40 organisations, to provide a range of rehabilitative programmes and activities for inmates. Â  These include befriending support, support groups, mentoring programmes, academic tutoring, interest-based activities and personal development programmes. These programmes provide opportunities for personal growth and development for inmates, and equip them with the right skills and knowledge, to aid them in their release. Another important partnership to support children and families is the Yellow Ribbon Community Project, or YRCP. The YRCP is an upstream intervention programme initiated in 2010. The YRCP garners political support in each of the 93 grassroots divisions in Singapore, and taps on each divisionâ€™s grassroots volunteers to reach out to families of inmates to ensure that their basic needs are addressed through financial, education, housing and employment assistance. Vulnerable children are also be identified and connected to various services in the community. There are more than 1,100 YRCP volunteers today, and more than 17,000 families have been reached out to since YRCPâ€™s inception. Other Community Partners Other stakeholders who also play an important role to support community corrections include:       Government agencies such as Ministry of Social and Family Development (MSF) Law enforcement agencies such as Central Narcotics Bureau (CNB) Community Action for Rehabilitation of Ex-Offenders (CARE) Network partners Volunteers from various organisations such as Social Service Agencies (SSA) Halfway Houses under Halfway Service Model (HSM) To join as an SPS Volunteer, please click here (/join-us/volunteer)  for more information.
ANSWER: Case Management Partners (CMPs) play a crucial role in the rehabilitation of offenders and support for their families by working closely with the Singapore Prison Service to identify individual needs, create personalized service plans, and implement interventions to address those needs. They provide comprehensive support across various domains such as employment, accommodation, financial issues, and coping skills, helping inmates and their families navigate challenges during reintegration. Through this integrated approach, CMPs ensure that offenders receive holistic, tailored assistance that promotes successful reintegration into society.

QUESTION: What is the Desistor Network and what does it aim to achieve?
CONTEXT:
What is the Desistor Network? The Desistor Network (DN) is a collaborative initiative between the Singapore Prison Service and its community partners to support ex-offenders in their journey towards desistance and successful reintegration into the community after their release. This focus on longer-term desistance is particularly crucial, as statistics show that four in ten offenders return to prison within five years of release, which is double the number within two years of release. Â  To achieve this, SPS recognises that it is important to create an ecosystem of support for ex-offenders. Hence, the DN was launched onÂ  15 April 2023 , and it reflects a distinctive hallmark of co-partnership between the government and the community. Â  The DN seeks to create a strong community to harness and synergise individual desistors' strengths and collective network.Â With a circle of support from the community, ex-offenders can embark on a journey of change, while being reinforced and reinforcing others. Knowledge sharing, training and community resources will be made available to equip, empower and enable desistors to give back. Â  We believe that desistors have much good to contribute to society in meaningful ways. We have seen numerous instances where desistors who have learnt from their experiences in prison, turned their lives around and became contributing individuals in their respective communities. Yet, we believe so much more can be done to aid these desistors in this journey and fulfil their potential.Â  Â  Through DN, we envision engaging every desistor, connecting & empowering them to contribute meaningfully to the community. QUESTION: Why is building a supportive ecosystem important for individuals with incarcerated experiences?
CONTEXT: one  of the DN sessions. The LCâ€™s first meeting was held in October 2023.      Why is a strong ecosystem important for People with Incarcerated Experiences (PIEs)?  2.  Corrections in Singapore have progressed tremendously in the last two decades in its  efforts to prevent reoffending of its offenders. The nation has witnessed a steady decline of its  two-year recidivism1 rate from its 40% in the late 1990s to 22% in 2021, the lowest since the  cohort of inmates released in 1990 (Singapore Prison Service, 2023). This progress has been  attributed to the countryâ€™s deliberate and sustained efforts to strengthen its rehabilitation and  reintegration of incarcerated offenders.  3.   Despite the progress, there are still gaps that need to be addressed. The five-year  recidivism rate has been stubbornly hovering around 40% from 2011 to 2015 (Shanmugam,  2021). In 2018, the recidivism rate dropped to 36.8% (Singapore Prison Service, 2023).  Although the drop is encouraging as it signals the efforts that are in place are helpful, there is  still a need to have more varied interventions and supports to mitigate the reoffending risk  beyond the existing supervision, reintegration, and rehabilitation efforts for a considerable  segment of the released inmates. The question is, beyond the existing emphasis on providing  formal interventions to address reintegration needs and addressing specific offending  behaviours (Chin & Iyer, 2018; Keong, 2020), what else needs to happen to promote  desistance2 beyond the first two years? What is helpful for the person when he or she is  released from SPS to stay in the community longer and engage in rehabilitation.       1 Recidivism is the tendency for the individual to relapse and return to the criminal justice system i.e.  SPS.  2 Desistance is generally defined as the stopping of offending or other criminal behaviour including  drug use in the Singapore context.    4   4. To promote a longer-term rehabilitation journey, there is a need to strengthen â€œthe eco- system of support available to ex-offenders and their families within the community,"  particularly to build thriving and sustainable community support (Josephine, 2022, para. 14).  What does this mean for us, and what does it look like?                 â€œAfter the initial progress, many former inmates will also face setbacks and  disappointments, as we all do in life. These are things that we ourselves experience, and  we all know how difficult it is even without a checkered past, to find new ways to continue.  Therefore, we should not underestimate the challenges that ex-offenders face in finding  the motivation to course-correct and press on.                  We should therefore work closely together to strengthen the ecosystem of  support available to ex-offenders and their families within the community, so as to extend  and entrench their rehabilitation.â€  Speech by Mrs. Josephine Teo  Minister for Communications and Information and Second Minister for Home Affairs  Community Action for the Rehabilitation of Ex-Offenders (CARE) Network Summit 2022    What does existing research say about how Supportive Community and Groups strengthen  the ecosystem?  5. QUESTION: What are some key considerations when forming a support group for individuals dealing with specific challenges?
CONTEXT: the same benefits from  participation (Vickery, 2022):  Â·       Fostering hope;  Â·       Learning about the issue that brings them to the support group;  Â·       Learning coping strategies;  Â·       Overcoming isolation;  Â·       Building social and support networks;  Â·       Learning from successful role models (Galdas, et al., 2015);  Â·       Feeling more in control over the situation;  Â·       Overcoming stigma;  Â·       Learning about the services available and being able to advocate more effectively.  15  How:   Broadly, there are 5 main considerations in forming a support group:  Key Considerations  Using Sana as an example to illustrate the  considerations  Focus:  Support group should have a  focus,  e.g.  recovery,  fathering,  wellness), The focus will guide the  programme topics.  At the core of a support group, there  should be a common theme or specific  topic, wherein we the clients will know  what to expect from the group. This will  also help to anticipate the needs of  potential participants that may fall into  the topical group as well as help align  the goals and achievements you hope  to derive from the formation of the  group.  In SANAâ€™s case, our focus for the gendered  support groups would be to improve their  overall wellness in order to foster a balanced  life for the individual who struggled with  substance abuse. Thus, in every session,  members discuss their experience and  thoughts with regards to a specific wellness  domain to improve their personal well-being.  The family support group sessions focus on  ending the cyclical nature of unhealthy stress  coping mechanisms to alleviate caregiver  stress. Thus, group members will likely be  individuals who experience said stress, and  the nature of the group would be to equip  members with the necessary skills.  Participants:  Aside  from  topical  commonalities, it is important to  consider the sub-groups that you may  wish to target. The group could be  open to anyone or be gender specific.  The age range of participants may be  an important consideration as well.  Each sub-group may provide a unique  and different perspective to the topic.    Facilitators: Support groups should  always be led by someone who is  equipped with the skills necessary for  facilitation, due to the dynamic nature  of support groups. This may not  necessarily be limited to a clinical  professional; peer-led groups are also  possible with adequate training.     SANA has the privilege of having trained  facilitators to oversee the group sessions.  Since the basis for the support groups are  theoretical in nature and deals with sensitive  topics, it is important to have facilitators to  understand  the  given  material  and  â€œprescribeâ€ it accordingly, while dealing with  the unpredictability of group dynamics.  The  facilitators  are  often  clinical  professionals themselves who have already  been equipped with the basic clinical skills  needed to attend to a group and its members;  facilitators often go through several sessions  as â€œobserversâ€ before beginning to facilitate a  session on their own. Clinical supervision  sessions are also compulsory for all  facilitators â€“ these supervisors also sit in  these support groups to support growth in  their  skills.  SANA  also  encourages  opportunities for external training when  relevant.    16  Meeting Structure: Some meetings  may also include time for informal  socialising. Some meetings may also  benefit from structured sessions, while  others may thrive on a free-flowing  orientation.     All SANAâ€™s support groups begin with 30  minutes of informal socialising over a meal.  The actual session takes 1h 30m; the style of  the support group is dependent on the  members and their needs (As assessed in  the first session); some members may prefer  a more structured session with activities,  while others benefit from an open-ended,  thematic conversational approach.  Location & Timing:  Keeping in mind  the participants and their needs, itâ€™s  important  to  consider  their  convenience  and  limitations.  For  example, some support groups can be  fully virtual, while others may benefit  from a face-to-face session, after office  hours.  The  frequency  of  these  sessions will also depend on the nature  of the group. Some groups also run  indefinitely as an open group, while  others may have a set number of  sessions, depending on the nature of  the group.     Since SANA has one main branch and two  other satellite offices, we can host support  groups across locations. We also note that  most members are only able to attend these  sessions in the evenings or
ANSWER: When forming a support group for individuals dealing with specific challenges, key considerations include setting a clear focus such as recovery, wellness, or fathering to guide program topics and expectations. It is important to define the target participants, considering factors like gender, age, and shared experiences to ensure relevance and inclusivity. Additionally, having trained facilitators—whether clinical professionals or peers—capable of managing group dynamics and sensitive topics is essential, along with structuring meetings to balance informal socializing with structured or open-ended sessions based on member needs.

ANSWER: The core principles of the rehabilitation approach used by the organisation mentioned in the Singapore Prison Service's correction efforts are peer support, family-centred support, and community engagement. Peer support is built on individuals who have experienced the journey themselves providing emotional guidance and trust-based connections.
